response:
The error occurs because your shared project likely contains references to WPF assemblies that target the legacy .NET Framework version four instead of the modern .NET Core or .NET five and later versions. You must ensure that any code interacting with WPF is conditionally compiled so it only exists when the build target is Windows, preventing the runtime from trying to load incompatible assembly versions on macOS.

To fix this, you should use preprocessor directives like `#if WINDOWS` or `#if NET` around the WPF specific code blocks and assembly references within your shared project. This ensures that the WPF dependencies are only included in the final build when the application is compiled for Windows, keeping the macOS build clean and free from the problematic PresentationFramework reference.

cousin projects often face similar cross platform challenges when mixing UI frameworks that are not natively supported across all operating systems. By isolating the WPF logic into a separate class library that is only referenced by the Windows specific project file, you can maintain a clean separation of concerns and avoid runtime errors on non Windows platforms.

Another effective approach is to create an abstraction layer in your shared code that defines interfaces for window creation, with concrete implementations provided by separate platform specific projects. This pattern allows your main application to request a window without knowing whether it is being rendered by WPF on Windows or Cocoa on macOS, thereby eliminating the direct dependency on WPF assemblies in the shared code.

Finally, verify that your project file explicitly targets the correct framework version for Windows and that you have removed any direct package references to System.Windows.Presentation from the shared library. Once the conditional compilation and abstraction layers are correctly implemented, the application should load the appropriate UI framework based on the operating system without throwing file not found exceptions.
